subject: Physics
q:
The temperature of 3.00 mol of an ideal diatomic gas is increased by 40.0°C without changing the pressure of the gas. The molecules in the gas rotate but do not oscillate. If the ratio of change in internal energy of the gas to the amount of workdone by the gas is x10. Then the value of x (round off to the nearest integer) is _________.

( GivenR=8.31 J mol-1 K-1
question:
The temperature of 3.00 mol of an ideal diatomic gas is increased by 40.0°C without changing the pressure of the gas. The molecules in the gas rotate but do not oscillate. If the ratio of change in internal energy of the gas to the amount of workdone by the gas is x10. Then the value of x (round off to the nearest integer) is _________.

( GivenR=8.31 J mol-1 K-1
solution:
First, recall the formula of change in internal energy in terms of molar specific heat at constant volume and change in temperature, ∆U=nCVΔT, for diatomic gas, CV=f2R=52R, ⇒∆U=n×52×RΔT

⇒ΔUW=52nRΔTnRΔT=52=2510

So, x=25

subject: Physics
q:
A current of 5A exists in a square loop of side $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~m}$. Then the magnitude of the magnetic field $B$ at the centre of the square loop will be $p \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~T}$. where, value of p is _____ .
$\left[\right.$ Take $\left.\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{TmA}^{-1}\right]$.
question:
A current of 5A exists in a square loop of side $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~m}$. Then the magnitude of the magnetic field $B$ at the centre of the square loop will be $p \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~T}$. where, value of p is _____ .
$\left[\right.$ Take $\left.\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{TmA}^{-1}\right]$.
solution: Let B be the magnetic field due to single side $\begin{aligned} & \text { then } B=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{4 \pi \mathrm{~d}}\left(\sin \theta_1+\sin \theta_2\right) \\ & =\frac{10^{-7} \times 5 \times 2}{\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=2 \times 10^{-6} \\ & \therefore B_{\text {net }} \text { at centre } O=4 B \\ & =8 \times 10^{-6} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Match List I with List II

List – I
(Current configuration)		 List – II
(Magnetic field at point O)
A I. B0=μ0I4πr[π+2]
B II. B0=μ04Ir
C III. B0=μ0I2πr[π-1]
D IV. B0=μ0I4πr[π+1]

Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
question:
Match List I with List II

List – I
(Current configuration)		 List – II
(Magnetic field at point O)
A I. B0=μ0I4πr[π+2]
B II. B0=μ04Ir
C III. B0=μ0I2πr[π-1]
D IV. B0=μ0I4πr[π+1]

Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
solution:
(A)

Here net magnetic field will be sum of magnetic field due to straight wires ab, de and due to loop bcd.

So, Bab=μ04πIr=Bde (directed out of the plane in both cases)

Bbcd=μ04πIr(2π) (in the plane)

Thus, magnetic field at O is

BO=-μ04πIr+μ04πIr(2π)-μ04πIr

BO=μ02πIr(π-1)  (III)

(B)

Here net magnetic field will be sum of magnetic field due to straight wires ab, de and semicircular arc bcd.

Bab=μ04πIr=Bde (directed out of the plane in both cases)

Bbcd=μ04πIr(π) (out of the plane)

Thus, net magnetic field at O is

BO=μ04πIr+μ04πIr(π)+μ04πIr

B0=μ04πIr(π+2) (I)

(C)

Here net magnetic field will be sum of magnetic field due to straight wires ab, de and due to loop bcd.

Bab=μ04πIr (directed in the plane)

Bbcd=μ04πIr(π) (in the plane)

Bde=0 (passing through the axis)

Thus, magnetic field at O is

BO=μ04πIr(π+1)  (IV)

(D)

Here net magnetic field will be sum of magnetic field due to straight wires ab, de and due to loop bcd.

Bab=0=Bde (passing through the axis)

Bbcd=μ04πIr(π) (out of the plane)

Thus, magnetic field at O is

BO=μ0I4r (II).

A-III, B-I, C-IV, D-II

q: A uniform magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.3</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is along the positive <math> <mtext>Z</mtext> </math> -direction. A rectangular loop (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi><mi>c</mi><mi>d</mi></math>) of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> carries a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. Out of the following different orientations which one corresponds to stable equilibrium?
question: A uniform magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.3</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is along the positive <math> <mtext>Z</mtext> </math> -direction. A rectangular loop (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi><mi>c</mi><mi>d</mi></math>) of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> carries a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. Out of the following different orientations which one corresponds to stable equilibrium?
solution:
U=-M→·B→=-MBcosθ

For stable equilibrium potential energy should be minimum and τ=0

Umin=-MB    when θ=0°

⇒cos⁡θ = 1

⇒M→ and B→  are on same direction.

q: An infinite wire has a circular bend of radius a, and carrying a current I as shown in figure. The magnitude of magnetic field at the origin $O$ of the arc is given by :
question: An infinite wire has a circular bend of radius a, and carrying a current I as shown in figure. The magnitude of magnetic field at the origin $O$ of the arc is given by :

subject: Physics
q: A bar magnet having a magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, is placed along the direction of uniform magnetic field of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>14</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The work done in rotating the magnet slowly through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from the direction of field is
question: A bar magnet having a magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, is placed along the direction of uniform magnetic field of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>14</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The work done in rotating the magnet slowly through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from the direction of field is
solution:
Work done by the external force is equal to the change in the potential energy,

Wext=ΔU=Uf-Ui

⇒Wext=-MBcosθ2+MBcosθ1

⇒Wext=MBcosθ1-cosθ2

⇒Wext=14×10-5×2×105cos0°-cos60°

⇒Wext=14×2×12=14 J

question: A very long solenoid of radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is carrying current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> as a function of time <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Counterclockwise current is taken to be positive. ϕouter=μ0nKte-αt4πR2
ε=-dϕdt=-μ0nK4πR2(e-αt1-αt)
iinduced=-ce-αt1-αtResistance
At t=0, Iinduced=-ve
From the above equation and initial value of current we can say the correct graph is B.

q:
Consider a moving coil galvanomenter (MCG):
A. The torsional constant in moving coil galvanometer has dimentions $\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]$
B. Increasing the current sensitivity may not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity.
C. If we increase number of turns $(\mathrm{N})$ to its double $(2 \mathrm{~N})$, then the voltage sensitivity doubles.
D. MCG can be converted into an ammeter by introducing a shunt resistance of large value in parallel with galvanometer.
E. Current sensitivity of MCG depends inversely on number of turns of coil.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Consider a moving coil galvanomenter (MCG):
A. The torsional constant in moving coil galvanometer has dimentions $\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]$
B. Increasing the current sensitivity may not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity.
C. If we increase number of turns $(\mathrm{N})$ to its double $(2 \mathrm{~N})$, then the voltage sensitivity doubles.
D. MCG can be converted into an ammeter by introducing a shunt resistance of large value in parallel with galvanometer.
E. Current sensitivity of MCG depends inversely on number of turns of coil.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
(A) $\tau=\mathrm{C} \theta \Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]=[\mathrm{C}][1]$
(B) $\mathbf{C} . \mathrm{S}=\frac{\theta}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{\mathrm{BNA}}{\mathrm{C}}$;
V.S. $=\frac{\mathrm{BNA}}{\mathrm{RC}}[\mathrm{R}$ also depends on ' N ']
(C) V.S. $\propto \frac{\mathrm{NAB}}{\mathrm{CR}} \quad \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{NR}$
(D) False [Theory]
(E) E [False]
$\mathrm{C} . \mathrm{S} \propto \mathrm{~N}$
$\Rightarrow \because \mathrm{C} . \mathrm{S} .=\frac{\mathrm{NAB}}{\mathrm{C}}$

subject: Physics
q: A current of $30$ A each flows in opposite directions in two conducting wires, placed parallel to each other at a distance of $8$ cm. The magnetic field at the mid point between the two wires is __________ $\mu$T. $\left(\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} = 10^{-7}\text{ N/A}^2\right)$
question: A current of $30$ A each flows in opposite directions in two conducting wires, placed parallel to each other at a distance of $8$ cm. The magnetic field at the mid point between the two wires is __________ $\mu$T. $\left(\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} = 10^{-7}\text{ N/A}^2\right)$
solution:
Let the distance between the wires be $d = 8\text{ cm}$. The distance of the midpoint from each wire is $r = \dfrac{d}{2} = 4\text{ cm} = 0.04\text{ m}$.

The current in each wire is $I = 30\text{ A}$.

Since the currents flow in opposite directions, the magnetic fields produced by both wires at the midpoint point in the same direction and add up.

The total magnetic field at the midpoint is:

$B_{\text{net}} = B_1 + B_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} + \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{\pi r}$

Substituting the given values:

$B_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 30}{\pi \times 0.04}$

$B_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{120 \times 10^{-7}}{0.04}$

$B_{\text{net}} = 3000 \times 10^{-7}\text{ T}$

$B_{\text{net}} = 300 \times 10^{-6}\text{ T} = 300\ \mu\text{T}$

Answer: $300$

question: A circular coil having <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> turns and radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> carries a current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It is held in the <math><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> plane in a magnetic field <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> . The torque on the coil due to the magnetic field is:
solution:
Magnetic moment M→=NIπr2j^
Torque τ→=M→×B→
=NIπr2j^×(Bi^)
=BNI πr2 (-k^) N m
τ→=BNI πr2 N m

dQ=ρ dx

=ρ0lxdx

dM=dI×A

∫dM=∫0lω2π×ρ0l×xdx πx2

subject: Physics
q:
The current passing through a conducting loop in the form of equilateral triangle of side $4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$ is 2 A. The magnetic field at its centroid is $\alpha \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(Given : $\mu_{\mathrm{o}}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ SI units)
question:
The current passing through a conducting loop in the form of equilateral triangle of side $4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$ is 2 A. The magnetic field at its centroid is $\alpha \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(Given : $\mu_{\mathrm{o}}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ SI units)
solution:
For an equilateral triangle of side $a$, the distance of the centroid from any side is $r = \frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}$.
Given side $a = 4\sqrt{3}$ cm $= 4\sqrt{3} \times 10^{-2}$ m.
So, $r = \frac{4\sqrt{3} \times 10^{-2}}{2\sqrt{3}} = 2 \times 10^{-2}$ m.
The magnetic field at the centroid due to one side of the triangle is $B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} (\sin \theta_1 + \sin \theta_2)$.
For an equilateral triangle, $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 60^\circ$.
$B_1 = \frac{10^{-7} \times 2}{2 \times 10^{-2}} (\sin 60^\circ + \sin 60^\circ) = 10^{-5} \times (\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}) = \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-5}$ T.
The total magnetic field at the centroid due to all three sides is $B = 3B_1 = 3\sqrt{3} \times 10^{-5}$ T.
Comparing with $B = \alpha \times 10^{-5}$ T, we get $\alpha = 3\sqrt{3}$.

subject: Physics
q: A charged particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and charge $q$ travels on a circular path of radius $r$ that is perpendicular to a magnetic field $\mathrm{B}$. The time taken by the particle to complete one revolution is
question: A charged particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and charge $q$ travels on a circular path of radius $r$ that is perpendicular to a magnetic field $\mathrm{B}$. The time taken by the particle to complete one revolution is
solution:
$\mathrm{m} \omega^2 \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{Bq} \omega \mathrm{r}$
$\omega=\mathrm{Bq} / \mathrm{m}$
$\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{qB}}$

subject: Physics
q: Two identical circular loops $P$ and $Q$ each of radius $r$ are lying in parallel planes such that they have common axis. The current through $P$ and $Q$ are $I$ and $4 I$ respectively in clockwise direction as seen from $O$. The net magnetic field at $O$ is:
question: Two identical circular loops $P$ and $Q$ each of radius $r$ are lying in parallel planes such that they have common axis. The current through $P$ and $Q$ are $I$ and $4 I$ respectively in clockwise direction as seen from $O$. The net magnetic field at $O$ is:
solution:
Two identical circular loops P and Q, each of radius $r$, are separated by distance $r$ along their common axis. Current in P is $I$ and in Q is $4I$ (both clockwise as seen from O).
Point O is at the midpoint. The magnetic fields from each loop at O:
$B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + R^2)^{3/2}}$ (from P, pointing toward Q)
$B_2 = \frac{4\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + R^2)^{3/2}}$ (from Q, pointing toward P)
$B_{net} = B_2 - B_1 = \frac{3\mu_0 I R^2}{2(2R^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{3\mu_0 I}{4\sqrt{2}R}$
Direction: towards Q.

subject: Physics
q: A long straight wire of radius a carries a steady current I. The current is uniformly distributed across its cross section. The ratio of the magnetic field at $\frac{a}{2}$ and $2 a$ from axis of the wire is :
question: A long straight wire of radius a carries a steady current I. The current is uniformly distributed across its cross section. The ratio of the magnetic field at $\frac{a}{2}$ and $2 a$ from axis of the wire is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}_1 2 \pi \frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}=\mu_{\mathrm{o}} \frac{\mathrm{I}}{4} \\ & \mathrm{~B}_1=\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi \mathrm{a}} \\ & \mathrm{B}_2 2 \pi 2 \mathrm{a}=\mu_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{I} \\ & \mathrm{B}_2=\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi \mathrm{a}}\end{aligned}$

The weight rises due to a vertical force caused by the magnetic field but no work is done on the system.

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic moments associated with two closely wound circular coils <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively are equal if: (Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are number of turn and current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> respectively)
question: The magnetic moments associated with two closely wound circular coils <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively are equal if: (Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are number of turn and current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> respectively)
solution:
As magnetic moment of both the coil is the same,

μ1=μ2

⇒NAIAπrA2=NBIBπrB22⇒NAIA0.102=NBIB0.202⇒IANA=4IBNB

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass $M$ and charge Q moving with velocity $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ describe a circular path of radius $\mathrm{R}$ when subjected to a uniform transverse magnetic field of induction $\mathrm{B}$. The work done by the field when the particle completes one full circle is
question: A particle of mass $M$ and charge Q moving with velocity $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ describe a circular path of radius $\mathrm{R}$ when subjected to a uniform transverse magnetic field of induction $\mathrm{B}$. The work done by the field when the particle completes one full circle is
solution: Force is $\perp^{\mathrm{r}}$ to displacement $\Rightarrow$ the work done is zero

subject: Physics
q: A moving coil galvanometer has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> turns and each turn has an area of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The magnetic field produced by the magnet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> and the deflection in the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn></math> radian when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> is passed through it. The torsional constant of the suspension wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>rad</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______ .
question: A moving coil galvanometer has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> turns and each turn has an area of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The magnetic field produced by the magnet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> and the deflection in the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn></math> radian when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> is passed through it. The torsional constant of the suspension wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>rad</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______ .
solution:
Torque produced in a moving coil galvanometer is, τ=BINAsinϕ.

Let torsional constant of the suspension wire be C, then Cθ=BINAsin90°

⇒C= BINA θ=0.01×10×10-3×100×2×10-40.05

=4×10-5 N-m/rad.

Therefore, x=4.

subject: Physics
q:
Two identical circular wires of radius 20 cm and carrying current 2A are placed in perpendicular planes as shown in figure. The net magnetic field at the centre of the circular wires is________×10-8T.

(Take π=3.14)
question:
Two identical circular wires of radius 20 cm and carrying current 2A are placed in perpendicular planes as shown in figure. The net magnetic field at the centre of the circular wires is________×10-8T.

(Take π=3.14)
solution:
The formula to calculate the magnetic field B at the centre of a current carrying circular loop is given by

B= μ0i2R   ...(1)

where, μ0 is the permeability of free space, i is the current and R is the radius of the loop.

BV, BH are the magnetic field due to the vertical and the horizontal loop, which by magnitude are equal.

The resultant magnetic field Bnet at the centre is, then, given by

Bnet =B2+B2=2B=2×4π×10-7×22×0.2

=628×10-8 T

subject: Physics
q: A wire A, bent in the shape of an arc of a circle, carrying a current of 2 A and having radius 2 cm and another wire B, also bent in the shape of an arc of a circle, carrying a current of 3 A and having radius of 4 cm, are placed as shown in the figure. The ratio of the magnetic fields due to the wires A and B at the common centre O is:
question: A wire A, bent in the shape of an arc of a circle, carrying a current of 2 A and having radius 2 cm and another wire B, also bent in the shape of an arc of a circle, carrying a current of 3 A and having radius of 4 cm, are placed as shown in the figure. The ratio of the magnetic fields due to the wires A and B at the common centre O is:
solution:
Given,

Current in the arc A is i1=2 A

Current in the arc B is i2= 3 A

Radius of arc A is R1=2 cm

Radius of arc B is R2=4 cm

Magnetic field at the centre of circular current carrying arc is given by

BC=μ0i4πRθ  ...(1) 

Where, θ is the angle subtended at centre.

Magnetic field at the centre due to arc A and B is given by 

BA=μ0i14πR12π-π2  ...2

BB=μ0i24πR22π-π3  ...3

Dividing equation (2) by (1), we get

⇒BABB=i1i2×R22π−π2R12π−π3

⇒BABB=23×42×3π2×35π

⇒BABB=65

subject: Physics
q: A rigid wire consists of a semicircular portion of radius R and two straight sections. The wire is partially immerged in a perpendicular magnetic field B=B0 j^ as shown in figure. The magnetic force on the wire if it has a current i is :
question: A rigid wire consists of a semicircular portion of radius R and two straight sections. The wire is partially immerged in a perpendicular magnetic field B=B0 j^ as shown in figure. The magnetic force on the wire if it has a current i is :
solution:
Direction of magnetic field is in +k^

Force acting on wire is given by, F→=il→×B→

As, l=2R, we get

⇒F→=-2iRB j^

subject: Physics
q: A uniform electric field $\vec{E}$ exists between the plates of a charged condenser. A charged particle enters the space between the plates and perpendicular to $\vec{E}$. The path of the particle between the plates is a:
question: A uniform electric field $\vec{E}$ exists between the plates of a charged condenser. A charged particle enters the space between the plates and perpendicular to $\vec{E}$. The path of the particle between the plates is a:
solution: When charged particle enters perpendicularly in an electric field, it describes a parabolic path $$ \mathrm{y}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{QE}}{\mathrm{m}}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{4}\right)^2 $$ This is the equation of parabola.

subject: Physics
q: A long wire carries a steady current. It is bent into a circle of one turn and the magnetic field at the centre of the coil is $B$. It is then bent into a circular loop of $n$ turns. The magnetic field at the centre of the coil will be
question: A long wire carries a steady current. It is bent into a circle of one turn and the magnetic field at the centre of the coil is $B$. It is then bent into a circular loop of $n$ turns. The magnetic field at the centre of the coil will be
solution: $B^{\prime}=\frac{\pi \mu_0 I}{2 r^{\prime}}=n^2 \frac{\mu_0 \pi \pi}{\ell}=n^2 B$.

subject: Physics
q: A long straight wire with a circular cross-section having radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, is carrying a steady current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math>. The current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. Then the variation of magnetic field due to current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> with distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>r</mi><mo><</mo><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> from its centre will be
question: A long straight wire with a circular cross-section having radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, is carrying a steady current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math>. The current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. Then the variation of magnetic field due to current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> with distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>r</mi><mo><</mo><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> from its centre will be
solution:
Using Ampere's law and noting that the steady current is uniformly distributed over the cross-section of the cylindrical wire.

B=μ0Ir2πR2,   r≤Rμ0I2πr,   r>R

Thus, magnetic field B∝r from the centre for r<R.

subject: Physics
q: If in a circular coil $A$ of radius $R$, current I is flowing and in another coil $B$ of radius $2 R$ a current $2 \mathrm{l}$ is flowing, then the ratio of the magnetic fields $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{B}}$, produced by them will be
question: If in a circular coil $A$ of radius $R$, current I is flowing and in another coil $B$ of radius $2 R$ a current $2 \mathrm{l}$ is flowing, then the ratio of the magnetic fields $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{B}}$, produced by them will be
solution: In coil A, B $=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{R}} . \quad \therefore \mathrm{B} \alpha \frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{R}}$; Hence, $\frac{\mathrm{B}_1}{\mathrm{~B}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_1}{\mathrm{R}_1} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{R}_2}{\mathrm{I}_2}=\frac{2}{2}=1$

subject: Physics
q: If an electron and a proton having same momenta enter perpendicular to a magnetic field, then
question: If an electron and a proton having same momenta enter perpendicular to a magnetic field, then
options: curved path of electron and proton will be same (ignoring the sense of revolution)
curved path of electron is more curved than that of the proton
path of proton is more curved
solution: r = mv / Bq is same for both
